refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time, and (2) actually achieving the measured KPI. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligation—enabling the cycle to execute within the time envelope—without prescribing how (e.g., speed optimization, path planning, operator assistance). The actual achievement of p95 ≤ CT_target is a verification criterion tied to duty cycle and site conditions, not a functional requirement of the system itself. This separation clarifies that the system must provide the necessary functional capacity; measurement and validation occur in the verification domain.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct cycle time trials under the agreed duty cycle and representative site conditions. Measure p95 cycle time across a statistically significant sample (e.g., ≥30 cycles). Confirm p95 ≤ CT_target. Verify that no functional bottleneck (e.g., load arm speed, travel speed, dump mechanism response) prevents the cycle from completing within the target window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall enable load acquisition within the load phase time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall enable travel (loaded and unloaded) within the travel phase time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall enable material dump within the dump phase time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.4: The NGHE solution shall minimize transition delays between load, travel, and dump phases.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose the load→travel→dump cycle into independent sub-processes (load, travel, dump) with explicit state transitions and timing budgets; allocate cycle time margin to each sub-process based on operational constraints and site conditions.

DP2: Implement a centralized cycle orchestration controller that sequences load, travel, and dump operations with real-time feedback loops to detect and compensate for delays; enforce hard cycle-time boundaries via adaptive process prioritization.

DP3: Design the system with parallel load preparation and travel readiness; overlap non-blocking operations (e.g., load staging while previous cycle travels) to reduce sequential cycle time without increasing individual sub-process duration.

DP4: Establish a predictive cycle-time model calibrated to site conditions (terrain, load variability, distance) and operational policies; use model-based scheduling to pre-allocate resources and detect cycle-time violations before they occur.

DP5: Implement a hybrid approach combining cycle decomposition (DP1) with real-time feedback control (DP2) and predictive scheduling (DP4); use feedback to refine the predictive model and adapt orchestration dynamically.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Implement a hybrid approach combining cycle decomposition (DP1) with real-time feedback control (DP2) and predictive scheduling (DP4); use feedback to refine the predictive model and adapt orchestration dynamically.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between cycle orchestration controller and individual sub-process implementations if controller logic is not abstracted via well-defined state/timing interfaces.
Dependency between predictive model accuracy and site-condition sensing; model drift if sensing is unreliable or site conditions change unexpectedly.
Feedback loop latency: if real-time feedback is delayed, adaptive orchestration may over-correct or under-correct, destabilizing cycle time.
Cross-process dependencies: if load sub-process duration varies widely, travel and dump timing budgets must be flexible; rigid allocation will cause coupling failures.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 is selected because it balances robustness (decomposition + feedback) with adaptability (predictive scheduling). Coupling risks are manageable via explicit interface contracts (state machines, timing budgets) and decoupled feedback loops. DP1 alone is brittle (no adaptation); DP2 alone risks oscillation; DP4 alone is fragile to model error. DP5 mitigates all three by using decomposition as the baseline, feedback to detect deviations, and prediction to anticipate them.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Site conditions (terrain, load variability, distance) are measurable and stable enough to calibrate a predictive model within acceptable confidence bounds (e.g., ±5% cycle time).

RISK: If site conditions are highly variable or unpredictable (e.g., random obstacles, load composition changes), the predictive model will degrade; mitigation: use feedback loop to continuously refine model; accept wider cycle-time variance.
ASSUMPTION: Real-time feedback sensors (position, load state, travel speed) are available and reliable with latency ≤ 10% of sub-process duration.
RISK: Sensor failures or high latency will degrade feedback control; mitigation: implement sensor redundancy and graceful degradation to open-loop orchestration.
ASSUMPTION: The cycle orchestration controller can be implemented with sufficient computational headroom to execute state transitions and feedback logic within hard real-time constraints.
RISK: If controller is overloaded, cycle-time violations will occur; mitigation: allocate sufficient compute margin and profile controller load under peak duty cycle.
ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined cycle time target CT_target is achievable given the physical constraints of load, travel distance, and dump operations (i.e., no fundamental physics violation).
RISK: If CT_target is infeasible, no DP will satisfy the FR; mitigation: validate feasibility early via physics-based lower-bound analysis.
ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not impose hard constraints that conflict with cycle-time optimization (e.g., mandatory wait times, speed limits).

RISK: If safety policies enforce delays that exceed the cycle-time budget, the FR cannot be satisfied; mitigation: engage safety stakeholders early to identify conflicts and negotiate acceptable trade-offs.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state the acceptance criteria for cycle-time variability (e.g., p95 ≤ CT_target, p99 ≤ CT_target + 5%) and the operational context (e.g., duty cycle profile, site-condition ranges, sensor availability). Additionally, clarify whether CT_target is a hard constraint (must always be met) or a statistical target (p95 acceptable). This will enable tighter DP evaluation and risk mitigation. Introduce a secondary FR: 'The NGHE solution shall provide visibility into cycle-time performance and root-cause diagnostics to support continuous improvement and troubleshooting.' This FR maps to DP: 'Implement telemetry and logging of cycle-time components (load duration, travel duration, dump duration, transition delays) with real-time dashboards and post-hoc analysis tools.' This DP is orthogonal to the primary cycle-time DP and supports operational excellence.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Orchestration engine integrating cycle decomposition, real-time feedback control, and predictive scheduling with dynamic adaptation logic

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives real-time sensor data and state feedback from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to refine predictive model and control loop

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outputs adapted orchestration commands and scheduling directives to Vehicle and Teleoperation based on hybrid control logic

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints from Safety Services; outputs safety-aware scheduling adjustments and alerts when predictive model detects risk

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicates orchestration state and feedback-refined predictions across distributed nodes if multi-agent coordination is required

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: NGHE Solution is the orchestration/control hub. DP5 (hybrid cycle decomposition + feedback + predictive scheduling) is fundamentally an orchestration strategy. Cycle decomposition (DP1) and predictive scheduling (DP4) are core NGHE responsibilities. Real-time feedback control (DP2) integrates naturally as a closed-loop refinement mechanism. Minor diffusion: feedback sensors reside in Vehicle/Teleoperation; safety constraints flow from Safety Services; but control logic and model adaptation remain centralized in NGHE.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical/execution platform, not an orchestration engine. DP5 requires system-level coordination logic, predictive modeling, and cross-subsystem scheduling—all beyond Vehicle's scope. Feedback from Vehicle sensors is input to DP5, not the locus of DP5 itself. Heavy diffusion: orchestration logic would scatter across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.

Poor fit: Teleoperation handles remote command and human-in-the-loop control, not system-level orchestration or predictive scheduling. DP5's hybrid approach requires global visibility and coordination across multiple subsystems. Teleoperation is a consumer of orchestration outputs, not their producer. Severe diffusion: predictive model and cycle decomposition logic would need to span multiple modules.

Poor fit: Safety Services enforces safety constraints and monitors compliance, not orchestration or predictive scheduling. DP5 is a control/scheduling strategy that must respect safety but is not itself a safety service. Containment would require Safety Services to own orchestration logic, violating separation of concerns. Extreme diffusion: core DP5 logic (cycle decomposition, feedback control, predictive scheduling) would be scattered across NGHE, Vehicle, and Teleoperation.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP5 (hybrid orchestration combining cycle decomposition, real-time feedback, and predictive scheduling) is fundamentally a system-level control strategy. NGHE Solution is the designated orchestration engine and the natural owner of this logic. Feedback from Vehicle and Teleoperation sensors flows inbound via Telemetry; safety constraints flow inbound via SafetyAlert; adapted tasking flows outbound via Tasking. This configuration maintains high cohesion within NGHE and minimizes diffusion by centralizing the hybrid control model and its refinement loop.
containment_risks: Latency: Real-time feedback control (DP2 component) may experience delays if NGHE Solution is geographically or architecturally distant from Vehicle/Teleoperation sensors. Mitigation: ensure Telemetry interface has low-latency guarantees; consider edge-local feedback loops if latency becomes critical.

Model complexity: Integrating cycle decomposition, feedback control, and predictive scheduling into a single orchestration engine risks creating a monolithic, difficult-to-test module. Mitigation: decompose DP5 internally into sub-modules (cycle manager, feedback controller, predictor) with clear internal interfaces, but keep ownership within NGHE.

Coupling to Safety Services: DP5 must respect safety constraints dynamically. If Safety Services changes its constraint model, NGHE must adapt. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert interface as a contract; use adapter pattern to decouple constraint representation.

Distributed coordination: If Vehicle or Teleoperation operate in disconnected or multi-agent scenarios, NGHE's centralized orchestration may become a bottleneck. Mitigation: use Network Link to replicate orchestration state; design DP5 to support eventual consistency.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 (hybrid orchestration combining cycle decomposition, real-time feedback control, and predictive scheduling with dynamic adaptation) directly addresses the FR requirement to enable execution of load→travel→dump cycle within customer-defined cycle time target. The DP is logically sound: decomposition breaks the cycle into manageable phases; real-time feedback refines execution; predictive scheduling anticipates bottlenecks and adapts orchestration. The hybrid approach is necessary to meet the p95 ≤ CT_target acceptance criterion under variable site conditions. No logical gaps detected.

diffusion_risks: Latency diffusion: Real-time feedback control (DP2 component of DP5) depends on low-latency Telemetry ingestion from Vehicle and Teleoperation. If NGHE Solution is architecturally or geographically distant, feedback loop delays may violate cycle time targets. Mitigation required: enforce Telemetry interface SLA (e.g., <100ms end-to-end latency); consider edge-local feedback loops for time-critical subsystems.

Model complexity diffusion: DP5 integrates three distinct control strategies (cycle decomposition, feedback control, predictive scheduling) into a single NGHE orchestration engine. Risk of monolithic design, difficult testing, and tight coupling between sub-strategies. Mitigation: internally decompose DP5 into sub-modules (CycleManager, FeedbackController, PredictiveScheduler) with explicit internal interfaces; maintain single ownership boundary but enforce internal modularity.

Safety constraint coupling diffusion: DP5 must dynamically respect safety constraints from Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface. Changes to constraint model or safety policies risk cascading updates to NGHE's orchestration logic. Mitigation: formalize SafetyAlert as a contract; use adapter/strategy pattern to decouple constraint representation from orchestration logic; version SafetyAlert interface.

Distributed coordination diffusion: If Vehicle or Teleoperation operate in multi-agent or disconnected scenarios, NGHE's centralized orchestration becomes a single point of coordination. Network Link interface must support state replication and eventual consistency. Risk: orchestration decisions may become stale or inconsistent across nodes. Mitigation: design DP5 to support distributed consensus or quorum-based scheduling; define Network Link protocol for state synchronization.

Sensor ownership diffusion: Feedback sensors physically reside in Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems, not in NGHE Solution. While control logic is centralized in NGHE, sensor calibration, health monitoring, and data quality assurance are diffused. Mitigation: establish clear ownership of sensor data pipeline; define Telemetry interface to include data quality metadata (confidence, staleness, validity flags).
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score for DP5. The orchestration engine is the natural and correct owner of hybrid cycle control logic. Cycle decomposition (DP1) and predictive scheduling (DP4) are core NGHE responsibilities; real-time feedback control (DP2) integrates as a closed-loop refinement mechanism. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and maintain clear input/output boundaries. Minor diffusion (0.08) is acceptable: feedback sensors reside in Vehicle/Teleoperation (input source, not logic owner); safety constraints flow from Safety Services (input constraint, not logic owner). Alternative allocations (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) show poor containment (0.22–0.35) and severe diffusion (0.65–0.78), confirming NGHE as the correct choice. Ownership boundary is clear and defensible.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions) is measurable and traceable to DP5 implementation. Verification approach: (1) Instrument NGHE Solution to log cycle decomposition phases, feedback control adjustments, and predictive scheduling decisions; (2) Collect telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation during field trials under representative duty cycles; (3) Compute p95 cycle time distribution and compare to CT_target; (4) Correlate cycle time variance to feedback control latency, predictive model accuracy, and safety constraint adjustments. Feasibility is high: NGHE Solution is the single point of orchestration, making end-to-end cycle time attribution straightforward. Risk: if cycle time target is not met, root cause analysis must distinguish between DP5 logic defects (e.g., poor scheduling heuristic) vs. IM realization defects (e.g., latency in Telemetry interface). Mitigation: define internal metrics within NGHE (e.g., feedback loop latency, predictor accuracy, scheduling efficiency) to isolate DP vs. IM issues.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear and unambiguous. FR is allocated to DP5 (hybrid orchestration strategy); DP5 is allocated to NGHE Solution (orchestration engine module). Interfaces are explicit: Telemetry (in), Tasking (out), SafetyAlert (inout), Network Link (inout). Worksite safety plan and operational policies are inputs to SafetyAlert interface, ensuring safety constraints are respected by DP5 logic. No ambiguity regarding which module owns orchestration responsibility. Traceability chain (FR → DP5 → NGHE Solution) is direct and defensible. Potential clarity gap: internal decomposition of DP5 into sub-modules (CycleManager, FeedbackController, PredictiveScheduler) is not yet formalized in the IM boundary definition. Recommendation: document internal module structure and interfaces to support detailed design and testing.

recommended_revisions: Formalize internal decomposition of NGHE Solution: Define sub-modules for cycle decomposition (CycleManager), real-time feedback control (FeedbackController), and predictive scheduling (PredictiveScheduler) with explicit internal interfaces. This reduces monolithic complexity and improves testability while maintaining single ownership boundary.

Define Telemetry interface SLA: Specify maximum end-to-end latency for feedback sensor data (e.g., <100ms p99) to ensure real-time feedback control (DP2 component) meets cycle time targets. Include data quality metadata (confidence, staleness, validity flags) to support feedback controller robustness.

Formalize SafetyAlert interface as a contract: Define safety constraint schema, versioning strategy, and adapter pattern to decouple constraint representation from orchestration logic. Ensure NGHE can dynamically adapt scheduling to new or changed safety policies without code changes.

Design Network Link protocol for distributed coordination: If multi-agent scenarios are anticipated, specify state replication, consensus mechanism, and eventual consistency guarantees for orchestration decisions across distributed NGHE nodes.

Establish sensor data ownership and quality assurance: Define which module (Vehicle, Teleoperation, or NGHE) owns sensor calibration, health monitoring, and data quality assurance. Ensure Telemetry interface includes provenance and quality metadata.

Add internal metrics to NGHE Solution: Instrument DP5 logic to log feedback loop latency, predictive model accuracy, scheduling efficiency, and safety constraint adjustments. These metrics enable root cause analysis if cycle time targets are not met and distinguish DP logic defects from IM realization defects.

Document cycle time attribution methodology: Define how p95 cycle time will be measured, which phases are included (load, travel, dump, idle), and how variance is attributed to feedback control, predictive scheduling, and safety constraints. This supports verification and troubleshooting.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. DP5 (hybrid orchestration) is logically sound, correctly allocated to NGHE Solution, and verification is feasible. However, the gate results explicitly identify that DP5 integrates three distinct control strategies (cycle decomposition, feedback control, predictive scheduling) and recommend formal internal decomposition into sub-modules (CycleManager, FeedbackController, PredictiveScheduler) to reduce monolithic complexity and improve testability. Additionally, safety criticality is high, and the diffusion risks (latency, model complexity, safety constraint coupling, distributed coordination, sensor ownership) require detailed sub-level design to mitigate. Proceeding to next-level decomposition of DP5 into sub-FRs is justified to: (1) formalize internal module structure and interfaces; (2) address safety-critical feedback control and constraint handling in detail; (3) establish clear ownership and interface contracts for sub-components; (4) enable independent verification of cycle decomposition, feedback control, and predictive scheduling logic.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Decompose load→travel→dump cycle into sequenced phases with phase-transition logic and phase-specific resource allocation constraints.

FR5.2: Implement real-time feedback control loop that ingests telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation, detects deviations from planned cycle phases, and dynamically adjusts orchestration commands to correct deviations and maintain cycle time targets.

FR5.3: Implement predictive scheduling engine that forecasts bottlenecks (e.g., travel time variance, dump site congestion) and proactively adjusts cycle phase timing and resource allocation to minimize p95 cycle time.

FR5.4: Enforce dynamic safety constraints from Safety Services (via SafetyAlert interface) such that orchestration decisions respect no-go zones, speed limits, equipment restrictions, and operational policies without blocking cycle execution.

FR5.5: Coordinate orchestration decisions across distributed Vehicle and Teleoperation agents (if multi-agent scenarios apply) via Network Link interface, ensuring state consistency and consensus on cycle phase transitions.

revise.why: DP5 is currently stated as a monolithic 'hybrid orchestration combining cycle decomposition, real-time feedback control, and predictive scheduling with dynamic adaptation.' To support next-level decomposition and reduce complexity diffusion risk, DP5 should be explicitly decomposed into sub-DPs corresponding to each sub-FR: DP5.1 (cycle phase decomposition and sequencing), DP5.2 (feedback control loop with deviation detection and correction), DP5.3 (predictive scheduling with bottleneck forecasting), DP5.4 (safety constraint enforcement via adapter/strategy pattern), DP5.5 (distributed coordination protocol). This formalization clarifies internal module boundaries, reduces monolithic risk, and enables independent verification of each sub-strategy.

assumptions: Cycle decomposition into load→travel→dump phases is sufficient to capture the operational structure; no additional phases (e.g., queue, maintenance) are required at this level.
Real-time feedback control can achieve <100ms end-to-end latency from sensor to orchestration command via Telemetry interface; if not, cycle time targets may not be achievable.

Predictive scheduling model (e.g., travel time, dump site availability) can be trained and maintained with sufficient accuracy to reduce p95 cycle time variance; model drift or data quality issues are managed separately.

Safety constraints from Safety Services are well-defined, versioned, and can be represented as a formal contract (SafetyAlert interface); ad-hoc or ambiguous safety policies will degrade orchestration performance.

Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems can provide telemetry data with quality metadata (confidence, staleness, validity flags) to support feedback controller robustness; raw sensor data without quality context is insufficient.
Multi-agent scenarios (distributed Vehicle or Teleoperation nodes) are anticipated; if single-agent operation is guaranteed, FR5.5 (distributed coordination) may be deferred or simplified.
open_risks: Latency diffusion: Feedback control loop depends on <100ms end-to-end Telemetry latency. If NGHE Solution is geographically or architecturally distant from Vehicle/Teleoperation, latency may exceed threshold and violate cycle time targets. Mitigation: enforce Telemetry interface SLA; consider edge-local feedback loops for time-critical subsystems.

Model complexity diffusion: DP5 integrates three control strategies. Risk of monolithic design, difficult testing, and tight coupling. Mitigation: formalize internal decomposition into sub-modules with explicit internal interfaces; maintain single ownership boundary but enforce internal modularity.

Safety constraint coupling diffusion: DP5 must dynamically respect safety constraints from Safety Services. Changes to constraint model risk cascading updates to orchestration logic. Mitigation: formalize SafetyAlert as a contract; use adapter/strategy pattern to decouple constraint representation from orchestration logic.

Distributed coordination diffusion: If Vehicle or Teleoperation operate in multi-agent scenarios, NGHE's centralized orchestration becomes a single point of coordination. Risk: orchestration decisions may become stale or inconsistent across nodes. Mitigation: design DP5.5 to support distributed consensus or quorum-based scheduling; define Network Link protocol for state synchronization.

Sensor ownership diffusion: Feedback sensors reside in Vehicle and Teleoperation, not NGHE. Sensor calibration, health monitoring, and data quality assurance are diffused. Mitigation: establish clear ownership of sensor data pipeline; define Telemetry interface to include data quality metadata.

Predictive model accuracy: Predictive scheduling (DP5.3) depends on accurate forecasting of travel time, dump site availability, and other bottlenecks. If model accuracy is poor or data is stale, predictive scheduling may not reduce p95 cycle time. Mitigation: define model accuracy targets; implement model monitoring and retraining; include model confidence in scheduling decisions.

Safety constraint conflicts: Safety constraints from Safety Services may conflict with cycle time optimization goals (e.g., speed limits reduce travel time efficiency). Risk: orchestration logic must balance safety and performance. Mitigation: define priority rules (safety always wins); formalize trade-off analysis in DP5.4 design.
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface specification: Define sensor data schema, latency SLA (<100ms p99), data quality metadata (confidence, staleness, validity flags), and error handling for missing or stale data.

SafetyAlert interface contract: Define safety constraint schema, versioning strategy, constraint types (no-go zones, speed limits, equipment restrictions), and adapter pattern for decoupling constraint representation from orchestration logic.

Network Link protocol specification: If multi-agent scenarios apply, define state replication mechanism, consensus algorithm, eventual consistency guarantees, and failure handling for distributed orchestration.
Cycle phase definition and sequencing logic: Document load, travel, dump phases with phase-transition conditions, phase-specific resource allocation constraints, and idle/queue handling.
Feedback control loop design: Specify deviation detection thresholds, correction strategies (e.g., speed adjustment, route replanning), feedback loop frequency, and latency budget allocation.

Predictive scheduling model: Define input features (historical cycle times, site conditions, equipment state), model type (e.g., regression, time-series forecasting), accuracy targets, and retraining strategy.
Internal module interfaces: Define CycleManager, FeedbackController, PredictiveScheduler sub-modules with explicit input/output contracts, call sequences, and error handling.

Cycle time measurement and attribution methodology: Define which phases are included (load, travel, dump, idle), how p95 is computed, and how variance is attributed to feedback control, predictive scheduling, and safety constraints.
Field trial plan: Define representative duty cycles, site conditions, acceptance criteria (p95 ≤ CT_target), and root cause analysis methodology for cycle time deviations.
